revisions.content: An Eastern [[Europe]]an country, the Ukraine borders the [[Black Sea]], [[Moldova]] [[Romania]], [[Hungary]], [[Slovakia]], [[Poland]] [[Belarus]] and [[Russia]]. The Ukraine has been fought over and subjugated for centuries; its 20th-century struggle for liberty is not yet complete. A short-lived independence from [[Russia]] (1917-1920) was followed by brutal [[Soviet]] rule that engineered two artificial famines (1921-22 and 1932-33) in which over 8 million died, and [[World War II]], in which [[German]] and [[Soviet]] armies were responsible for some 7 million more deaths. Although independence was attained in 1991 with the dissolution of the [[USSR]], true freedom remains elusive as many of the former [[Soviet]] elite remain entrenched, stalling efforts at economic reform, privatization, and civic liberties. The capital is [[Kyiv]] (pronounced Kyyiv), and was formerly known as Kiev (Kiyev). From the [[CIA World Factbook]] 2000. [[eo:Ukrajno]][[fr:Ukraine]][[pl:Ukraina]]
[[Image:Ukraine_flag_medium.png]]
An Eastern [[Europe]]an country, Ukraine -- not "the" Ukraine -- borders the [[Black Sea]], [[Moldova]] [[Romania]], [[Hungary]], [[Slovakia]], [[Poland]] [[Belarus]] and [[Russia]]. Ukraine has been fought over and subjugated for centuries. It had short-lived independence from [[Russia]] (1917-1920). Parts of Ukraine were conquered by Poland after Polish war against Soviet Union . Brief independence was followed by brutal [[Soviet]] rule that engineered two artificial famines (1921-22 and 1932-33) in which over eight million died. In [[World War II]] the [[German]] and [[Soviet]] armies were responsible for seven million more deaths. Although independence was attained in 1991 with the dissolution of the [[USSR]], true freedom remains elusive as many of the former [[Soviet]] elite remain entrenched, stalling efforts at economic reform, privatization, and civic liberties. The capital is [[Kyiv]] (pronounced Kyyiv), and was formerly known as [[Kiev]] (Kiyev). From the [[CIA World Factbook]] 2000. Not Wikified. * [[Geography of Ukraine]] * [[Demographics of Ukraine]] * [[Politics of Ukraine]] * [[Economy of Ukraine]] * [[Communications in Ukraine]] * [[Transportation in Ukraine]] * [[Military of Ukraine]] * [[Foreign relations of Ukraine]]

[[de:Ukraine]][[eo:Ukrajno]][[fr:Ukraine]][[pl:Ukraina]]
[[Image:Ukraine_flag_medium.png]]
An Eastern [[Europe]]an country, '''Ukraine''' -- not "the" Ukraine -- borders the [[Black Sea]], [[Moldova]] [[Romania]], [[Hungary]], [[Slovakia]], [[Poland]] [[Belarus]] and [[Russia]]. Ukraine has been fought over and subjugated for centuries. It had short-lived independence from [[Russia]] (1917-1920). Parts of Ukraine were conquered by Poland after Polish war against Soviet Union . Brief independence was followed by brutal [[Soviet]] rule that engineered two artificial famines (1921-22 and 1932-33) in which over eight million people died. There is still much debate over the topic though. In [[World War II]] the [[Germany|German]] and to a much lesser extent, [[Soviet]] armies were responsible for seven million deaths. Although independence was attained in 1991 with the dissolution of the [[USSR]], true freedom remains elusive as many of the former [[Soviet]] elite remain entrenched, stalling efforts at economic reform, privatization, and civic liberties. The capital is [[Kyiv]] (pronounced Kyyiv), and was formerly known as [[Kiev]] (Kiyev). * [[History of Ukraine]] * [[Geography of Ukraine]] * [[Demographics of Ukraine]] * [[Politics of Ukraine]] * [[Economy of Ukraine]] * [[Communications in Ukraine]] ===Miscellanious topics=== * [[Transportation in Ukraine]] * [[Military of Ukraine]] * [[Foreign relations of Ukraine]] * [[List of famous Ukranians]] * [[Tourism in Ukraine]] ''Much of this text was originally from the [[CIA World Factbook]] 2000.''

[[de:Ukraine]] [[eo:Ukrajno]] [[fr:Ukraine]] [[pl:Ukraina]] [[sv:Ukraina]] '''Ukraine''' is a country in eastern [[Europe]] which borders the [[Black Sea]] to the south, the [[Russian Federation]] to the east, [[Belarus]] to the north and [[Poland]], [[Slovakia]], [[Hungary]], [[Romania]] and [[Moldova]] to the west.

== History == ''Main article: [[History of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine was the centre of the first [[Slavic]] state, [[Kievan Rus]], which during the [[10th century|10th]] and [[11th century|11th centuries]] was the largest and most powerful state in [[Europe]] and laid the foundation for Ukrainian nationalism through subsequent centuries. Weakened by internecine quarrels and [[Mongol]] invasions, Kievan Rus was incorporated into the Grand Duchy of [[Lithuania]] and eventually into the [[Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth]]. During the mid-[[17th century]] an autonomous Ukrainian state, the [[Cossack Hetmanate]], was established that remained for well over a century despite continuous [[Muscovy|Muscovite]] pressure. During the latter part of the [[18th century]], central and eastern parts of Ukrainian ethnographic territory were absorbed by the [[Russian Empire]], with the west becoming part of [[Austria-Hungary]]. Following the [[Russian Revolution]] in [[1917]], Ukraine was briefly independent until [[1920]]. The country was reconquered and experienced a [[Soviet Union|Soviet]] rule that engineered two artificial [[famine]]s ([[1921]]-[[1922]] and [[1932]]-[[1933]]) in which over 8 million died. In [[World War II]], German and Soviet armies were responsible for some 7 million more deaths. Renewed independence was achieved in [[1991]] with the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]] and Ukraine was a founding member of the [[Commonwealth of Independent States]]. == Politics == ''Main article: [[Politics of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is a parliamentary democracy with separate [[executive]], [[judiciary|judicial]], and [[legislative]] branches. The [[president]], elected by popular vote for a five-year term, nominates the [[prime minister]] as well as the rest of the [[cabinet (government)|cabinet]], who must be confirmed by the parliament. The Ukrainian [[parliament]] is the [[unicameral]] Supreme Council or ''Verkhovna Rada'' which holds 450 seats, 225 of which are allocated on a proportional basis to those parties that gain 4% or more of the national electoral vote; the other 225 members are elected by popular vote in single-mandate constituencies. All members serve four-year terms and the parliament initiates legislation, ratifies international agreements, and approves the budget. == Regions == ''Main article: [[Regions of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is subdivided into 24 regions (''oblasti'', singular - ''oblast'''), 1 autonomous republic (''avtomnaya respublika'') in the [[Crimea]], and 2 municipalities (''mista'', singular - ''misto'') with regional status, marked by a *:
* [[Cherkasy region|Cherkasy]] * [[Chernihiv region|Chernihiv]] * [[Chernivtsi region|Chernivtsi]] * [[Dnipropetrovs'k region|Dnipropetrovs'k]] * [[Donets'k region|Donets'k]] * [[Ivano-Frankivs'k region|Ivano-Frankivs'k]] * [[Kharkiv region|Kharkiv]] * [[Kherson region|Kherson]] * [[Khmel'nyts'kyy region|Khmel'nyts'kyy]] * [[Kirovohrad region|Kirovohrad]] * [[Kiev]] * * [[Kiev region]] * [[Luhans'k region|Luhans'k]] * [[L'viv region|L'viv]] * [[Mykolayiv region|Mykolayiv]] * [[Odessa region|Odessa]] * [[Poltava region|Poltava]] * [[Republic of Crimea]] * [[Rivne region|Rivne]] * [[Sevastopol]] * * [[Sumy region|Sumy]] * [[Ternopil' region|Ternopil']] * [[Vinnytsya pregion|Vinnytsya]] * [[Volyn region|Volyn]] * [[Zakarpattya region|Zakarpattya]] * [[Zaporizhzhya region|Zaporizhzhya]] * [[Zhytomyr region|Zhytomyr]]
[[Image:Up-map.jpg]] == Geography == ''Main article: [[Geography of Ukraine]]'' The Ukrainian landscape consists mostly of fertile plains or [[steppes]] and plateaus, crossed by rivers such as the [[Dniepr]], [[Donets]], [[Dnister]] and the Southern [[Bug (Rivers)|Bug]] as they flow down into the [[Black Sea]] and the smaller [[Sea of Azov]]. To the southwest the [[river delta|delta]] of the [[Danube]] forms the border with Romania. Mountains are found only in the western range of the [[Carpathian Mountains]], of which the highest is the [[Hora Hoverla]] at 2,061 m, and in the [[Crimea]]n peninsula in the extreme south along the coast. Ukraine has a [[temperate climate|temperate]] continental [[climate]], though a more mediterranean clime is found on the southern Crimean coast. [[Precipitation]] is disproportionately distributed; it's highest in the west and north and lesser in the east and southeast. Winters vary from cool along the Black Sea to cold farther inland; summers are warm across the greater part of the country, but generally hot in the south. == Economy == ''Main article: [[Economy of Ukraine]]'' Formerly an important [[agriculture|agricultural]] and industrial region of the [[Soviet Union]], Ukraine now depends on [[Russia]] for most energy supplies, especially [[natural gas]], and the lack of significant structural reform have made the Ukrainian economy vulnerable to external shocks. After [[1991]] the government liberalised most prices and erected a legal framework for [[privatisation]], but widespread resistance to reform within the government and the legislature soon stalled reform efforts and led to some backtracking. Output by [[1999]] had fallen to less than 40% the 1991 level. Loose monetary policies pushed [[inflation]] to [[hyperinflation]]ary levels in late [[1993]]. The current government has pledged to reduce the number of government agencies, streamline the regulatory process, create a legal environment to encourage entrepreneurs, and enact a comprehensive [[tax]] overhaul. Reforms in the more politically sensitive areas of structural reform and land privatisation are still lagging. Outside institutions - particularly the [[IMF]] - have encouraged Ukraine to quicken the pace and scope of reforms and have threatened to withdraw financial support. The [[Gross Domestic Product|GDP]] in [[2000]] showed strong export-based growth of 6% - the first growth since independence - and industrial production grew 12.9%. The economy continued to expand in [[2001]] as real GDP rose 9% and industrial output grew by over 14%. Growth was undergirded by strong domestic demand and growing consumer and investor confidence. == Demographics == ''Main article: [[Demographics of Ukraine]]'' Ethnic Ukrainians make up about 75% of the total population, ethnic [[Russia]]ns number about 20%. The industrial regions in the east and southeast are the most heavily populated, and about 70% of the population is urban. [[Ukrainian language|Ukrainian]] (the official state language) and [[Russian language|Russian]] are the principal languages and although Russian is very widely spoken most of the population identifies Ukrainian as their native language. Other minorities include small groups of [[Belarus]]sians, [[Moldova]]ns, Crimean [[Tatar]]s, [[Bulgaria]]ns, [[Hungary|Hungarians]], [[Romania]]ns, [[Poland|Poles]] and [[Jews]]. The dominant religions are the [[Ukrainian Orthodox Church]], an [[Eastern Orthodox]] church, and the [[Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church]], which practices Orthodox rites but recognises the [[Pope]] as head of the church. The largest part of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church belongs to the [[Moscow Patriarchy]]; however, following Ukrainian independence a separate [[Kiev Patriarchy]] also was established, which declared independence from Moscow. In addition to these, there also is a [[Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church]], as well as smaller [[Roman Catholic]], [[Protestant]] and [[Judaism|Jewish]] communities. == Culture == ''Main article: [[Culture of Ukraine]]'' * [[List of Ukrainians]] == Miscellaneous topics == * [[Communications in Ukraine]] * [[Transportation in Ukraine]] * [[Military of Ukraine]] * [[Foreign relations of Ukraine]] * [[Tourism in Ukraine]] * [[List of cities in Ukraine]] == External Links == * [http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en Government Portal] - Official governmental portal * [http://www.rada.gov.ua Verhovna Rada] - Official parliamentary site (in Ukranian)

Outside institutions - particularly the [[IMF]] - have encouraged Ukraine to quicken the pace and scope of reforms and have threatened to withdraw financial support. The [[Gross Domestic Product|GDP]] in [[2000]] showed strong export-based growth of 6% - the first growth since independence - and industrial production grew 12.9%. The economy continued to expand in [[2001]] as real GDP rose 9% and industrial output grew by over 14%. Growth was undergirded by strong domestic demand and growing consumer and investor confidence. == Demographics == ''Main article: [[Demographics of Ukraine]]'' Ethnic [[Ukrainians]] make up about 75% of the total population, ethnic [[Russia]]ns number about 20%. The industrial regions in the east and southeast are the most heavily populated, and about 70% of the population is urban. [[Ukrainian language|Ukrainian]] (the official state language) and [[Russian language|Russian]] are the principal languages and although Russian is very widely spoken most of the population identifies Ukrainian as their native language. Other minorities include small groups of [[Belarus]]sians, [[Moldova]]ns, Crimean [[Tatar]]s, [[Bulgaria]]ns, [[Hungary|Hungarians]], [[Romania]]ns, [[Poland|Poles]] and [[Jews]]. === Religions === The dominant religions are the [[Ukrainian Orthodox Church]], an [[Eastern Orthodox]] church, and the [[Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church]], which practices eastern Christian rites but recognises the [[Pope]] as head of the church. The largest part of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church belongs to the [[Moscow Patriarchy]]; however, following Ukrainian independence a separate [[Kiev Patriarchy|Kyiv Patriarchy]] also was established, which declared independence from Moscow. In addition to these, there also is a [[Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church]], as well as smaller [[Roman Catholic]], [[Protestant]], [[Judaism|Jewish]] and [[Moslem]] communities. == Culture == ''Main article: [[Culture of Ukraine]]'' * [http://pages.prodigy.net/l.hodges/ukraine.htm Ukrainian Language, Culture and Travel Page] * [[List of Ukrainians|List of famous Ukrainians]] == Miscellaneous topics == * [[Communications in Ukraine]] * [[Transportation in Ukraine]] * [[Military of Ukraine]] * [[Foreign relations of Ukraine]] * [[Tourism in Ukraine]] * [[List of cities in Ukraine]] == External links == * [http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en Government Portal] - Official governmental portal * [http://www.rada.gov.ua Verkhovna Rada] - Official parliamentary site (in Ukranian) * Web cam shots for selected cities across Ukraine - [http://www.ukrtelecom.ua/en/offers/web_cam/] ----

== History == ''Main article: [[History of Ukraine]]'' Current ukrainian territory was a southern part of the first [[Slavic]] state, [[Kievan Rus]]. The state (not the city) was founded by Swedish [[Vikings]], the so called [[Varangian]]s or [[Kievan Rus'|Rus']] (see [[Rurik]]), who gave Russia its first powerful dynasty the [[Rurik]] dynasty. During the [[10th century|10th]] and [[11th century|11th centuries]] these [[Vikings]] in the east created the largest and most powerful state in [[Europe]] and, ironically, laid the foundation for Ukrainian nationalism through subsequent centuries. Weakened by internecine quarrels and [[Mongol]] invasions, soutern parts of Kievan Rus were incorporated into the Grand Duchy of [[Lithuania]] and eventually into the [[Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth]]; northern parts formed independent [[Muscovy]] (Moscow Rus). During the mid-[[17th century]] an autonomous Ukrainian state, the [[Cossack Hetmanate]], was established that remained for well over a century despite continuous [[Muscovy|Muscovite]] pressure. During the latter part of the [[18th century]], central and eastern parts of Ukrainian ethnographic territory were absorbed by the [[Russian Empire]], with the west becoming part of [[Austria-Hungary]]. Following the [[Russian Revolution]] in [[1917]], Ukraine was briefly independent until [[1920]]. The country was reconquered and experienced a [[Soviet Union|Soviet]] rule that engineered two artificial [[famine]]s ([[1921]]-[[1922]] and [[1932]]-[[1933]]) in which over 8 million died. In [[World War II]], German and Soviet armies were responsible for some 7 million more deaths. Renewed independence was achieved in [[1991]] with the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]] and Ukraine was a founding member of the [[Commonwealth of Independent States]]. == Politics == ''Main article: [[Politics of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is a parliamentary democracy with separate [[executive]], [[judiciary|judicial]], and [[legislative]] branches. The [[president]], elected by popular vote for a five-year term, nominates the [[prime minister]] as well as the rest of the [[cabinet (government)|cabinet]], who must be confirmed by the parliament. The Ukrainian [[parliament]] is the [[unicameral]] Supreme Council or ''Verkhovna Rada'' which holds 450 seats, 225 of which are allocated on a proportional basis to those parties that gain 4% or more of the national electoral vote; the other 225 members are elected by popular vote in single-mandate constituencies. All members serve four-year terms and the parliament initiates legislation, ratifies international agreements, and approves the budget. The national flag of Ukraine represents the blue sky over the wheat fields of the [[steppe]]s. The two colours have long been used as banners for the Ukrainian people. == Regions == ''Main article: [[Regions of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is subdivided into 24 regions (''oblasti'', singular - ''oblast''), 1 autonomous republic (''avtomna respublika'') in the [[Crimea]], and 2 municipalities (''mista'', singular - ''misto'') with regional status, marked by a *:
* [[Cherkasy region|Cherkasy]] * [[Chernihiv region|Chernihiv]] * [[Chernivtsi region|Chernivtsi]] * [[Dnipropetrovs'k region|Dnipropetrovs'k]] * [[Donets'k region|Donets'k]] * [[Ivano-Frankivs'k region|Ivano-Frankivs'k]] * [[Kharkiv region|Kharkiv]] * [[Kherson region|Kherson]] * [[Khmel'nyts'kyy region|Khmel'nyts'kyy]] * [[Kirovohrad region|Kirovohrad]] * [[Kiev|Kyiv]] * * [[Kiev region|Kyiv region]] * [[Luhans'k region|Luhans'k]] * [[L'viv region|L'viv]] * [[Mykolayiv region|Mykolayiv]] * [[Odessa region|Odessa]] * [[Poltava region|Poltava]] * [[Republic of Crimea]] * [[Rivne region|Rivne]] * [[Sevastopol]] * * [[Sumy region|Sumy]] * [[Ternopil' region|Ternopil']] * [[Vinnytsya pregion|Vinnytsya]] * [[Volyn region|Volyn]] * [[Zakarpattya region|Zakarpattya]] * [[Zaporizhzhya region|Zaporizhzhya]] * [[Zhytomyr region|Zhytomyr]]
[[Image:Up-map.jpg|Map of Ukraine]]
Map == Geography == ''Main article: [[Geography of Ukraine]]'' The Ukrainian landscape consists mostly of fertile plains or [[steppes]] and plateaus, crossed by rivers such as the [[Dniepr]], [[Donets]], [[Dnister]] and the Southern [[Bug (Rivers)|Bug]] as they flow down into the [[Black Sea]] and the smaller [[Sea of Azov]]. To the southwest the [[river delta|delta]] of the [[Danube]] forms the border with Romania. Mountains are found only in the western range of the [[Carpathian Mountains]], of which the highest is the [[Hora Hoverla]] at 2,061 m, and in the [[Crimea]]n peninsula in the extreme south along the coast. Ukraine has a [[temperate climate|temperate]] continental [[climate]], though a more mediterranean clime is found on the southern Crimean coast. [[Precipitation]] is disproportionately distributed; it's highest in the west and north and lesser in the east and southeast. Winters vary from cool along the Black Sea to cold farther inland. Summers are warm across the greater part of the country, but generally hot in the south. == Economy == ''Main article: [[Economy of Ukraine]]'' Formerly an important [[agriculture|agricultural]] and industrial region of the [[Soviet Union]], Ukraine now depends on [[Russia]] for most energy supplies, especially [[natural gas]], and the lack of significant structural reform have made the Ukrainian economy vulnerable to external shocks. After [[1991]] the government liberalised most prices and erected a legal framework for [[privatisation]], but widespread resistance to reform within the government and the legislature soon stalled reform efforts and led to some backtracking. Output by [[1999]] had fallen to less than 40% the 1991 level. Loose monetary policies pushed [[inflation]] to [[hyperinflation]]ary levels in late [[1993]]. The current government has pledged to reduce the number of government agencies, streamline the regulatory process, create a legal environment to encourage entrepreneurs, and enact a comprehensive [[tax]] overhaul. Reforms in the more politically sensitive areas of structural reform and land privatisation are still lagging. Outside institutions - particularly the [[IMF]] - have encouraged Ukraine to quicken the pace and scope of reforms and have threatened to withdraw financial support. The [[Gross Domestic Product|GDP]] in [[2000]] showed strong export-based growth of 6% - the first growth since independence - and industrial production grew 12.9%. The economy continued to expand in [[2001]] as real GDP rose 9% and industrial output grew by over 14%. Growth was undergirded by strong domestic demand and growing consumer and investor confidence. == Demographics == ''Main article: [[Demographics of Ukraine]]'' Ethnic [[Ukrainians]] make up about 75% of the total population, ethnic [[Russia]]ns number about 20%. The industrial regions in the east and southeast are the most heavily populated, and about 70% of the population is urban. [[Ukrainian language|Ukrainian]] (the official state language) and [[Russian language|Russian]] are the principal languages and although Russian is very widely spoken most of the population identifies Ukrainian as their native language. Other minorities include small groups of [[Belarus]]sians, [[Moldova]]ns, Crimean [[Tatar]]s, [[Bulgaria]]ns, [[Hungary|Hungarians]], [[Romania]]ns, [[Poland|Poles]] and [[Jews]]. === Religions === The dominant religions are the [[Ukrainian Orthodox Church]], an [[Eastern Orthodox]] church, and the [[Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church]], which practices eastern Christian rites but recognises the [[Pope]] as head of the church. The largest part of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church belongs to the [[Moscow Patriarchy]]; however, following Ukrainian independence a separate [[Kiev Patriarchy|Kyiv Patriarchy]] also was established, which declared independence from Moscow. In addition to these, there also is a [[Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church]], as well as smaller [[Roman Catholic]], [[Protestant]], [[Judaism|Jewish]] and [[Moslem]] communities. == Culture == ''Main article: [[Culture of Ukraine]]'' * [http://pages.prodigy.net/l.hodges/ukraine.htm Ukrainian Language, Culture and Travel Page] * [[List of Ukrainians|List of famous Ukrainians]] == Miscellaneous topics == * [[Communications in Ukraine]] * [[Transportation in Ukraine]] * [[Military of Ukraine]] * [[Foreign relations of Ukraine]] * [[Tourism in Ukraine]] * [[List of cities in Ukraine]] == External links == * [http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en Government Portal] - Official governmental portal * [http://www.rada.gov.ua Verkhovna Rada] - Official parliamentary site (in Ukranian) * Web cam shots for selected cities across Ukraine - [http://www.ukrtelecom.ua/en/offers/web_cam/] ----

* [[Cherkasy region|Cherkasy]] * [[Chernihiv region|Chernihiv]] * [[Chernivtsi region|Chernivtsi]] * [[Dnipropetrovs'k region|Dnipropetrovs'k]] * [[Donets'k region|Donets'k]] * [[Ivano-Frankivs'k region|Ivano-Frankivs'k]] * [[Kharkiv region|Kharkiv]] * [[Kherson region|Kherson]] * [[Khmel'nyts'kyy region|Khmel'nyts'kyy]] * [[Kirovohrad region|Kirovohrad]] * [[Kiev|Kyiv]] * * [[Kiev region|Kyiv region]] * [[Luhans'k region|Luhans'k]] * [[L'viv region|L'viv]] * [[Mykolayiv region|Mykolayiv]] * [[Odessa region|Odessa]] * [[Poltava region|Poltava]] * [[Republic of Crimea]] * [[Rivne region|Rivne]] * [[Sevastopol]] * * [[Sumy region|Sumy]] * [[Ternopil' region|Ternopil']] * [[Vinnytsya pregion|Vinnytsya]] * [[Volyn region|Volyn]] * [[Zakarpattya region|Zakarpattya]] * [[Zaporizhzhya region|Zaporizhzhya]] * [[Zhytomyr region|Zhytomyr]]
[[Image:Up-map.jpg|Map of Ukraine]]
Map == Geography == ''Main article: [[Geography of Ukraine]]'' The Ukrainian landscape consists mostly of fertile plains or [[steppes]] and plateaus, crossed by rivers such as the [[Dniepr]], [[Donets]], [[Dnister]] and the Southern [[Bug (Rivers)|Bug]] as they flow down into the [[Black Sea]] and the smaller [[Sea of Azov]]. To the southwest the [[river delta|delta]] of the [[Danube]] forms the border with Romania. Mountains are found only in the western range of the [[Carpathian Mountains]], of which the highest is the [[Hora Hoverla]] at 2,061 m, and in the [[Crimea]]n peninsula in the extreme south along the coast. Ukraine has a [[temperate climate|temperate]] continental [[climate]], though a more mediterranean clime is found on the southern Crimean coast. [[Precipitation]] is disproportionately distributed; it's highest in the west and north and lesser in the east and southeast. Winters vary from cool along the Black Sea to cold farther inland. Summers are warm across the greater part of the country, but generally hot in the south. == Economy == ''Main article: [[Economy of Ukraine]]'' Formerly an important [[agriculture|agricultural]] and industrial region of the [[Soviet Union]], Ukraine now depends on [[Russia]] for most energy supplies, especially [[natural gas]], and the lack of significant structural reform have made the Ukrainian economy vulnerable to external shocks. After [[1991]] the government liberalised most prices and erected a legal framework for [[privatisation]], but widespread resistance to reform within the government and the legislature soon stalled reform efforts and led to some backtracking. Output by [[1999]] had fallen to less than 40% the 1991 level. Loose monetary policies pushed [[inflation]] to [[hyperinflation]]ary levels in late [[1993]]. The current government has pledged to reduce the number of government agencies, streamline the regulatory process, create a legal environment to encourage entrepreneurs, and enact a comprehensive [[tax]] overhaul. Reforms in the more politically sensitive areas of structural reform and land privatisation are still lagging. Outside institutions - particularly the [[IMF]] - have encouraged Ukraine to quicken the pace and scope of reforms and have threatened to withdraw financial support. The [[Gross Domestic Product|GDP]] in [[2000]] showed strong export-based growth of 6% - the first growth since independence - and industrial production grew 12.9%. The economy continued to expand in [[2001]] as real GDP rose 9% and industrial output grew by over 14%. Growth was undergirded by strong domestic demand and growing consumer and investor confidence. == Demographics == ''Main article: [[Demographics of Ukraine]]'' Ethnic [[Ukrainians]] make up about 75% of the total population, ethnic [[Russia]]ns number about 20%. The industrial regions in the east and southeast are the most heavily populated, and about 70% of the population is urban. [[Ukrainian language|Ukrainian]] (the official state language) and [[Russian language|Russian]] are the principal languages and although Russian is very widely spoken most of the population identifies Ukrainian as their native language. Other minorities include small groups of [[Belarus]]sians, [[Moldova]]ns, Crimean [[Tatar]]s, [[Bulgaria]]ns, [[Hungary|Hungarians]], [[Romania]]ns, [[Poland|Poles]] and [[Jews]]. The dominant religions are the [[Ukrainian Orthodox Church]], an [[Eastern Orthodox]] church, and the [[Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church]], which practices eastern Christian rites but recognises the [[Pope]] as head of the church. The largest part of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church belongs to the [[Moscow Patriarchy]]; however, following Ukrainian independence a separate [[Kiev Patriarchy|Kyiv Patriarchy]] also was established, which declared independence from Moscow. In addition to these, there also is a [[Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church]], as well as smaller [[Roman Catholic]], [[Protestant]], [[Judaism|Jewish]] and [[Moslem]] communities. == Culture == ''Main article: [[Culture of Ukraine]]'' * [http://pages.prodigy.net/l.hodges/ukraine.htm Ukrainian Language, Culture and Travel Page] * [[List of Ukrainians|List of famous Ukrainians]] *[[Music of Ukraine]] == Miscellaneous topics == * [[Communications in Ukraine]] * [[Transportation in Ukraine]] * [[Military of Ukraine]] * [[Foreign relations of Ukraine]] * [[Tourism in Ukraine]] * [[List of cities in Ukraine]] == External links == * [http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en Government Portal] - Official governmental portal * [http://www.rada.gov.ua Verkhovna Rada] - Official parliamentary site (in Ukranian) * [http://www.ukrtelecom.ua/en/offers/web_cam/ Web cam shots for selected cities across Ukraine]

== History == ''Main article: [[History of Ukraine]]'' Current Ukrainian territory was a southern part and the site of the capital of the first [[Slavic]] state, [[Kievan Rus]]. The state was founded by Swedish [[Vikings]], the so called [[Varangian]]s or [[Kievan Rus'|Rus']] (see [[Rurik]]), who gave Ukraine its first powerful dynasty, the [[Rurik]] dynasty. During the [[10th century|10th]] and [[11th century|11th centuries]] these Vikings in the east created the largest and most powerful state in [[Europe]] and, ironically, laid the foundation for Ukrainian nationalism through subsequent centuries. Kievan Rus' was weakened by internecine quarrels and [[Mongol]] invasions. Southern parts of Kievan Rus' gave rise to [[Volhynia|principalities of Halych and Volodymyr-Volynskii]], which were merged, separated and eventually incorporated into the [[Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth]]. Northern parts formed Mongol dependent [[Muscovy]] (Moscow Rus). The Ukrainian province of [[Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth|the Commonwealth]] had considerable amount of freedoms and autonomy. During the mid-[[17th century]] an autonomous Ukrainian state, the [[Cossack Hetmanate]], was established in the central Ukraine that remained for well over a century despite continuous [[Muscovy|Muscovite]] pressure. However, the rest of Ukraine was split between Poland and Russia. During the latter part of the [[18th century]], central and eastern parts of Ukrainian ethnographic territory were annexed by the [[Russian Empire]], with the west becoming part of [[Austria-Hungary]]. Following the [[Russian Revolution]] in [[1917]], Ukraine was briefly independent until [[1920]]. The country was reconquered and experienced a [[Soviet Union|Soviet]] rule that engineered two genocidal [[famine]]s ([[1921]]-[[1922]] and [[1932]]-[[1933]]) - known as the Holodomor - in which over 8 million died. In [[World War II]], [[Nazi Germany|Nazi German]] and [[Soviet union|Soviet]] armies were responsible for some 7 million more deaths. However, after the WWII, Ukraine borders where extended to the West by shifting Poland, see [[Curzon line]], and to the East by acquiring [[Crimea]]. Renewed independence was achieved in [[1991]] with the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]] and Ukraine was a founding member of the [[Commonwealth of Independent States]]. == Politics == ''Main article: [[Politics of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is a parliamentary democracy with separate [[executive]], [[judiciary|judicial]], and [[legislative]] branches. The [[president]], elected by popular vote for a five-year term, nominates the [[prime minister]] as well as the rest of the [[cabinet (government)|cabinet]], who must be confirmed by the parliament. The Ukrainian [[parliament]] is the [[unicameral]] Supreme Council or ''Verkhovna Rada'' which holds 450 seats, 225 of which are allocated on a proportional basis to those parties that gain 4% or more of the national electoral vote; the other 225 members are elected by popular vote in single-mandate constituencies. All members serve four-year terms and the parliament initiates legislation, ratifies international agreements, and approves the budget. The national flag of Ukraine represents the blue sky over the wheat fields of the [[steppe]]s. The two colours have long been used as banners for the Ukrainian people. == Regions == ''Main article: [[Regions of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is subdivided into 24 regions (''oblasti'', singular - ''oblast''), 1 autonomous republic (''avtomna respublika'') in the [[Crimea]], and 2 municipalities (''mista'', singular - ''misto'') with regional status, marked by a *:
* [[Cherkasy region|Cherkasy]] * [[Chernihiv region|Chernihiv]] * [[Chernivtsi region|Chernivtsi]] * [[Dnipropetrovs'k region|Dnipropetrovs'k]] * [[Donets'k region|Donets'k]] * [[Ivano-Frankivs'k region|Ivano-Frankivs'k]] * [[Kharkiv region|Kharkiv]] * [[Kherson region|Kherson]] * [[Khmel'nyts'kyy region|Khmel'nyts'kyy]] * [[Kirovohrad region|Kirovohrad]] * [[Kiev]] * * [[Kiev region]] * [[Luhans'k region|Luhans'k]] * [[L'viv region|L'viv]] * [[Mykolayiv region|Mykolayiv]] * [[Odessa region|Odessa]] * [[Poltava region|Poltava]] * [[Republic of Crimea]] * [[Rivne region|Rivne]] * [[Sevastopol]] * * [[Sumy region|Sumy]] * [[Ternopil' region|Ternopil']] * [[Vinnytsya pregion|Vinnytsya]] * [[Volyn region|Volyn]] * [[Zakarpattya region|Zakarpattya]] * [[Zaporizhzhya region|Zaporizhzhya]] * [[Zhytomyr region|Zhytomyr]]

== History == ''Main article: [[History of Ukraine]]'' The current territory of what is today the nation of Ukraine was a southern part of the first [[Slavic]] state, [[Kievan Rus]]. The capital of Ukraine also was the capital of the state of Rus' one thousand years ago. The state called Kievan Rus' was founded by [Vikings]], or so-called [[Varangian]]s, from the area that later became Sweden. These ScandanavianVikings later became assimilated to the local population of Rus' ([[Ruthenian]]/Ukrainian) and gave Ukraine its first powerful dynasty, the [[Rurik]] dynasty. During the [[10th century|10th]] and [[11th century|11th centuries]] the territory of Ukraine became the center of the most powerful state in [[Europe]]--laying the foundation for Ukrainian nationalism through subsequent centuries. Eventually, Kievan Rus' became weakened by internal quarrels and [[Mongol]] invasions. On Ukrainian territory, the state of Kievan Rus' was succeeded by the [[Principalities of Halych and Volodymyr-Volynskii]], which were merged, separaed and eventually conquered by Poland and Lithuania (see the [[Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth]]). During the mid-[[17th century]] a Ukrainian state, the [[Cossack Hetmanate]], was established in the central Ukraine, which remained for well over a century despite continuous [[Muscovy|Muscovite]] pressure. However, eventually Ukraine was split between the neighboring states of Poland and Russia. During the latter part of the [[18th century]], the central and eastern parts of Ukrainian ethnographic territory were annexed by the [[Russian Empire]], with the west becoming part of [[Austria-Hungary]] after the partitions of Poland. Following the [[Russian Revolution]] in [[1917]], Ukraine was briefly independent until [[1920]]. The country was reconquered by (Soviet) Russia, and came under [[Soviet Union|Soviet]] rule. During this period, the Ukrainian nation experienced two[[famine]]s ([[1921]]-[[1922]] and [[1932]]-[[1933]]) - the second of which was deliberate, and termed the "Holodomor" - in which many millions died. (scholarly estimates range from 4 to 10 million dead). In [[World War II]], [[Nazi Germany|Nazi German]] and [[Soviet union|Soviet]] armies were responsible for some 7 million more deaths. After the WWII, the borders of then Soviet Ukraine were extended to the West, (see [[Curzon line]]). Later, during Soviet rule in 1954, Ukraine acquired [[Crimea]]. Renewed independence was achieved in [[1991]] with the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]] and Ukraine was a founding member of the [[Commonwealth of Independent States]]. == Politics == ''Main article: [[Politics of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is a parliamentary democracy with separate [[executive]], [[judiciary|judicial]], and [[legislative]] branches. All members serve four-year terms and the parliament initiates legislation, ratifies international agreements, and approves the budget. == History == ''Main article: [[History of Ukraine]]'' The current territory of what is today the nation of Ukraine was a southern part of the first [[Slavic]] state, [[Kievan Rus']]. Its capital was [[Kyiv]], the capital of the modern Ukraine. Kievan Rus' was founded by [[Vikings]], or so-called [[Varangian]]s, from the area that later became Sweden. Varangians later became assimilated to the local population of Rus' ([[Ruthenian]]/Ukrainian) and gave Ukraine its first powerful dynasty, the [[Rurik]] dynasty. During the [[10th century|10th]] and [[11th century|11th centuries]] the territory of Ukraine became the center of the most important state in [[Europe]]--laying the foundation for Ukrainian national identity through subsequent centuries. Eventually, Kievan Rus' became weakened by internal quarrels and [[Mongol]] invasions. On Ukrainian territory, the state of Kievan Rus' was succeeded by the principalities of [[Halych]] and [[Volhynia|Volodymyr-Volynskii]], which were merged, separated and eventually conquered by Poland and Lithuania (see the [[Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth]]). During the mid-[[17th century]] a Ukrainian state, the [[Cossack Hetmanate]], was established in central Ukraine, which remained for well over a century despite continuous [[Muscovy|Muscovite]] pressure. However, eventually Ukraine was split between the neighboring states of Poland and Russia. During the latter part of the [[18th century]], the central and eastern parts of Ukrainian ethnographic territory were annexed by the [[Russian Empire]], with the west becoming part of [[Austria-Hungary]] after the partitions of Poland. Following the [[Russian Revolution]] in [[1917]], Ukraine was briefly independent until [[1920]]. The country was reconquered by (Soviet) Russia, and came under [[Soviet Union|Soviet]] rule. During this period, the Ukrainian nation experienced two [[famine]]s ([[1921]]-[[1922]] and [[1932]]-[[1933]]) - the second of which was deliberate, and termed the "Holodomor" - in which many millions died (scholarly estimates range from 4 to 10 million dead). In [[1941]] the [[Great Patriotic War]] swept over Ukraine as the German invaders and their [[Axis]] allies crushed the defending [[Red Army]]. In the encirclement battle of [[Kiev]] ([[Hero City]]) alone, more than 660,000 Soviet troops were taken captive. Initially the Germans were warmly received as "liberators" by the Ukrainian population on the borders. Soon, however, the Germans began their bloody regime of genocide, killing and deporting Jews and Ukrainian civilians and burning down entire villages. Total civilian losses during the War and German occupation in Ukraine are estimated at 7 million. After the WWII, the borders of then Soviet Ukraine were extended to the West, (see [[Curzon line]]). Later, during Soviet rule in 1954, Ukraine acquired [[Crimea]]. Renewed independence was achieved in [[1991]] with the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]] and Ukraine was a founding member of the [[Commonwealth of Independent States]]. == Politics == ''Main article: [[Politics of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is a parliamentary democracy with separate [[executive]], [[judiciary|judicial]], and [[legislative]] branches. The [[president]], elected by popular vote for a five-year term, nominates the [[prime minister]] as well as the rest of the [[cabinet (government)|cabinet]], who must be confirmed by the parliament. The Ukrainian [[parliament]] is the [[unicameral]] Supreme Council or ''Verkhovna Rada'' which holds 450 seats, 225 of which are allocated on a proportional basis to those parties that gain 4% or more of the national electoral vote; the other 225 members are elected by popular vote in single-mandate constituencies. All members serve four-year terms and the parliament initiates legislation, ratifies international agreements, and approves the budget. The national flag of Ukraine represents the blue sky over the wheat fields of the [[steppe]]s. The two colours have long been used as banners for the Ukrainian people. == Regions == ''Main article: [[Regions of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is subdivided into 24 regions (''oblasti'', singular - ''oblast''), 1 autonomous republic (''avtomna respublika'') in the [[Crimea]], and 2 municipalities (''mista'', singular - ''misto'') with regional status, marked by a *:
* [[Cherkasy region|Cherkasy]] * [[Chernihiv region|Chernihiv]] * [[Chernivtsi region|Chernivtsi]] * [[Dnipropetrovs'k region|Dnipropetrovs'k]] * [[Donets'k region|Donets'k]] * [[Ivano-Frankivs'k region|Ivano-Frankivs'k]] * [[Kharkiv region|Kharkiv]] * [[Kherson region|Kherson]] * [[Khmel'nyts'kyy region|Khmel'nyts'kyy]] * [[Kirovohrad region|Kirovohrad]] * [[Kiev]] * * [[Kiev region]] * [[Luhans'k region|Luhans'k]] * [[L'viv region|L'viv]] * [[Mykolayiv region|Mykolayiv]] * [[Odessa region|Odessa]] * [[Poltava region|Poltava]] * [[Republic of Crimea]] * [[Rivne region|Rivne]] * [[Sevastopol]] * * [[Sumy region|Sumy]] * [[Ternopil' region|Ternopil']] * [[Vinnytsya pregion|Vinnytsya]] * [[Volyn region|Volyn]] * [[Zakarpattya region|Zakarpattya]] * [[Zaporizhzhya region|Zaporizhzhya]] * [[Zhytomyr region|Zhytomyr]]
[[Image:Up-map.jpg|Map of Ukraine]]
Map == Geography == ''Main article: [[Geography of Ukraine]]'' The Ukrainian landscape consists mostly of fertile plains or [[steppes]] and plateaus, crossed by rivers such as the [[Dniepr]], [[Donets]], [[Dnister]] and the Southern [[Bug (Rivers)|Bug]] as they flow down into the [[Black Sea]] and the smaller [[Sea of Azov]]. To the southwest the [[river delta|delta]] of the [[Danube]] forms the border with Romania. Mountains are found only in the western range of the [[Carpathian Mountains]], of which the highest is the [[Hora Hoverla]] at 2,061 m, and in the [[Crimea]]n peninsula in the extreme south along the coast. Ukraine has a [[temperate climate|temperate]] continental [[climate]], though a more mediterranean clime is found on the southern Crimean coast. [[Precipitation]] is disproportionately distributed; it's highest in the west and north and lesser in the east and southeast. Winters vary from cool along the Black Sea to cold farther inland. Summers are warm across the greater part of the country, but generally hot in the south. == Economy == ''Main article: [[Economy of Ukraine]]'' Formerly an important [[agriculture|agricultural]] and industrial region of the [[Soviet Union]], Ukraine now depends on [[Russia]] for most energy supplies, especially [[natural gas]], and the lack of significant structural reform have made the Ukrainian economy vulnerable to external shocks. After [[1991]] the government liberalised most prices and erected a legal framework for [[privatisation]], but widespread resistance to reform within the government and the legislature soon stalled reform efforts and led to some backtracking. Output by [[1999]] had fallen to less than 40% the 1991 level. Loose monetary policies pushed [[inflation]] to [[hyperinflation]]ary levels in late [[1993]]. The current government has pledged to reduce the number of government agencies, streamline the regulatory process, create a legal environment to encourage entrepreneurs, and enact a comprehensive [[tax]] overhaul. Reforms in the more politically sensitive areas of structural reform and land privatisation are still lagging. Outside institutions - particularly the [[IMF]] - have encouraged Ukraine to quicken the pace and scope of reforms and have threatened to withdraw financial support. The [[Gross Domestic Product|GDP]] in [[2000]] showed strong export-based growth of 6% - the first growth since independence - and industrial production grew 12.9%. The economy continued to expand in [[2001]] as real GDP rose 9% and industrial output grew by over 14%. Growth was undergirded by strong domestic demand and growing consumer and investor confidence. == Demographics == ''Main article: [[Demographics of Ukraine]]'' Ethnic [[Ukrainians]] make up about 75% of the total population, ethnic [[Russia]]ns number about 20%. The industrial regions in the east and southeast are the most heavily populated, and about 70% of the population is urban. [[Ukrainian language|Ukrainian]] (the official state language) and [[Russian language|Russian]] are the principal languages and although Russian is very widely spoken most of the population identifies Ukrainian as their native language. Other minorities include small groups of [[Belarussian]]s, [[Moldovan]]s, [[Crimea]]n [[Tatars]], [[Bulgarians]], [[Magyars|Hungarians]], [[Romanians]], [[Poles]] and [[Jews]]. The dominant religions are the [[Ukrainian Orthodox Church]], an [[Eastern Orthodox]] church, and the [[Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church]], which practices eastern Christian rites but recognises the Roman [[Pope]] as head of the church. The largest part of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church belongs to the [[Moscow Patriarchy]]; however, following Ukrainian independence a separate [[Kiev Patriarchy]] also was established, which declared independence from Moscow. In addition to these, there also is a [[Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church]], as well as smaller [[Roman Catholic]], [[Protestant]], [[Judaism|Jewish]] and [[Moslem]] communities. == Culture == ''Main article: [[Culture of Ukraine]]'' * [http://pages.prodigy.net/l.hodges/ukraine.htm Ukrainian Language, Culture and Travel Page] * [[List of Ukrainians|List of famous Ukrainians]] *[[Music of Ukraine]] == Miscellaneous topics == * [[Communications in Ukraine]] * [[Transportation in Ukraine]] * [[Military of Ukraine]] * [[Foreign relations of Ukraine]] * [[Tourism in Ukraine]] * [[List of cities in Ukraine]] * [[Chernobyl accident]] * [[Julian Marchlewski|Polish Autonomous Region]] == External links == * [http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en Government Portal] - Official governmental portal * [http://www.rada.gov.ua Verkhovna Rada] - Official parliamentary site (in Ukranian) * [http://www.ukrtelecom.ua/en/offers/web_cam/ Web cam shots for selected cities across Ukraine] {{msg:Commonwealth_of_Independent_States}}

== History == ''Main article: [[History of Ukraine]]'' The current territory of what is today the nation of Ukraine was a southern part of the first [[Slavic]] state, [[Kievan Rus']]. Its capital was [[Kyiv]], the capital of the modern Ukraine. Kievan Rus' was founded by [[Vikings]], or so-called [[Varangian]]s, from the area that later became Sweden. Varangians later became assimilated to the local population of Rus' ([[Ruthenian]]/Ukrainian) and gave Ukraine its first powerful dynasty, the [[Rurik]] dynasty. During the [[10th century|10th]] and [[11th century|11th centuries]] the territory of Ukraine became the center of the most important state in [[Europe]]--laying the foundation for Ukrainian national identity through subsequent centuries. Eventually, Kievan Rus' became weakened by internal quarrels and [[Mongol]] invasions. On Ukrainian territory, the state of Kievan Rus' was succeeded by the principalities of [[Halych]] and [[Volhynia|Volodymyr-Volynskii]], which were merged, separated and eventually conquered by Poland and Lithuania (see the [[Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth]]). During the mid-[[17th century]] a Ukrainian state, the [[Cossack Hetmanate]], was established in central Ukraine, which remained for well over a century despite continuous [[Muscovy|Muscovite]] pressure. However, eventually Ukraine was split between the neighboring states of Poland and Russia. During the latter part of the [[18th century]], the central and eastern parts of Ukrainian ethnographic territory were annexed by the [[Russian Empire]], with the west becoming part of [[Austria-Hungary]] after the [[Partitions of Poland|partitions of Poland]]. Following the [[Russian Revolution]] in [[1917]], Ukraine was briefly independent, but not united, until [[1920]]. By [[1922]] it was split between [[Poland]] and [[Soviet Union]]. The Soviet Ukraine experienced two [[famine]]s ([[1921]]-[[1922]] and [[1932]]-[[1933]]) - the second of which was deliberate, and termed the "[[Holodomor]]" - in which many millions died (scholarly estimates range from 4 to 10 million dead). At the onset of the [[World War II]], in [[1939]], the [[Soviet Union]] invaded [[Poland]] and incorporated the Westen Ukraine into the [[Ukrainian SSR]]. In [[1941]] the German invaders and their [[Axis]] allies crushed the [[Red Army]]. In the encirclement battle of [[Kiev]], acclaimed by the Soviets as a "[[Hero City]]," more than 660,000 Soviet troops were taken captive. Initially, the Germans were warmly received as "liberators" by the Ukrainian population on the borders. Soon, however, the Germans began their bloody regime of genocide, killing and deporting Jews and Ukrainian civilians and burning down entire villages, leading many Ukrainians to conclude that Nazi rule was even worse than the Soviet regime which had killed 4 to 10 million of the Ukrainian nation, albeit over a longer period of time. Total civilian losses during the War and German occupation in Ukraine are estimated at 7 million, including over half a million Jews shot and killed by the [[Einsatzgruppen]]. Of the estimated 11 million Soviet troops who fell in battle against the Nazis, about a fourth (2.7 million) were ethnic Ukranians. Thus, the Ukrainian nation is distinguished as the first nation to fight the Axis powers during WW II in [[Carpatho-Ukraine]] and one that gave up perhaps the greatest amount of people during the WW II, counting both Soviet and Nazi occupations. After the WWII, the borders of then Soviet Ukraine were extended to the West, (see [[Curzon line]]), uniting most Ukrainians under one political state. In 1954, [[Crimea]] was transferred from [[RSFSR]] to Ukraine (Crimea has no continuous land bridge to the Russian Federation.) This decision of [[Nikita Khrushchev]], intended to commerate the 300th anniversary of the controversial [[Treaty of Pereyaslav]], seen in Soviet historiography as the 'union of two fraternal peoples', led to tensions between Russia and Ukraine after the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]]. Renewed independence was achieved in [[1991]] with the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]] and Ukraine was a founding member of the [[Commonwealth of Independent States]]. == Politics == ''Main article: [[Politics of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is a parliamentary democracy with separate [[executive]], [[judiciary|judicial]], and [[legislative]] branches. The [[president]], elected by popular vote for a five-year term, nominates the [[prime minister]] as well as the rest of the [[cabinet (government)|cabinet]], who must be confirmed by the parliament. The Ukrainian [[parliament]] is the [[unicameral]] Supreme Council or ''Verkhovna Rada'' which holds 450 seats, 225 of which are allocated on a proportional basis to those parties that gain 4% or more of the national electoral vote; the other 225 members are elected by popular vote in single-mandate constituencies. All members serve four-year terms and the parliament initiates legislation, ratifies international agreements, and approves the budget. The national flag of Ukraine represents the blue sky over the wheat fields of the [[steppe]]s. The two colors have long been used as banners for the Ukrainian people. == Regions == ''Main article: [[Regions of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is subdivided into 24 regions (''oblasti'', singular - ''oblast''), 1 autonomous republic (''avtomna respublika'') in the [[Crimea]], and 2 municipalities (''mista'', singular - ''misto'') with regional status, marked by a *:

* [[Cherkasy region|Cherkasy]] * [[Chernihiv region|Chernihiv]] * [[Chernivtsi region|Chernivtsi]] * [[Dnipropetrovs'k region|Dnipropetrovs'k]] * [[Donets'k region|Donets'k]] * [[Ivano-Frankivs'k region|Ivano-Frankivs'k]] * [[Kharkiv region|Kharkiv]] * [[Kherson region|Kherson]] * [[Khmel'nyts'kyy region|Khmel'nyts'kyy]] * [[Kirovohrad region|Kirovohrad]] * [[Kiev]] * * [[Kiev region]] * [[Luhans'k region|Luhans'k]] * [[L'viv region|L'viv]] * [[Mykolayiv region|Mykolayiv]] * [[Odessa region|Odessa]] * [[Poltava region|Poltava]] * [[Republic of Crimea]] * [[Rivne region|Rivne]] * [[Sevastopol]] * * [[Sumy region|Sumy]] * [[Ternopil' region|Ternopil']] * [[Vinnytsya pregion|Vinnytsya]] * [[Volyn region|Volyn]] * [[Zakarpattya region|Zakarpattya]] * [[Zaporizhzhya region|Zaporizhzhya]] * [[Zhytomyr region|Zhytomyr]]
== Geography == ''Main article: [[Geography of Ukraine]]'' [[Image:Up-map.png|thumb|200px|Map of Ukraine]] The Ukrainian landscape consists mostly of fertile plains or [[steppes]] and plateaus, crossed by rivers such as the [[Dniepr]], [[Donets]], [[Dnister]] and the Southern [[Bug (Rivers)|Bug]] as they flow down into the [[Black Sea]] and the smaller [[Sea of Azov]]. To the southwest the [[river delta|delta]] of the [[Danube]] forms the border with Romania. Mountains are found only in the western range of the [[Carpathian Mountains]], of which the highest is the [[Hora Hoverla]] at 2,061 m, and in the [[Crimea]]n peninsula in the extreme south along the coast. Ukraine has a [[temperate climate|temperate]] continental [[climate]], though a more [[mediterranean climate|mediterranean clime]] is found on the southern Crimean coast. [[precipitation (meteorology)|Precipitation]] is disproportionately distributed; it's highest in the west and north and lesser in the east and southeast. Winters vary from cool along the Black Sea to cold farther inland. Summers are warm across the greater part of the country, but generally hot in the south. == Economy == ''Main article: [[Economy of Ukraine]]'' Formerly an important [[agriculture|agricultural]] and industrial region of the [[Soviet Union]], Ukraine now depends on [[Russia]] for most energy supplies, especially [[natural gas]], and the lack of significant structural reform have made the Ukrainian economy vulnerable to external shocks. After [[1991]] the government liberalised most prices and erected a legal framework for [[privatisation]], but widespread resistance to reform within the government and the legislature soon stalled reform efforts and led to some backtracking. Output by [[1999]] had fallen to less than 40% the [[1991]] level. Loose monetary policies pushed [[inflation]] to [[hyperinflation]]ary levels in late [[1993]]. The current government has pledged to reduce the number of government agencies, streamline the regulatory process, create a legal environment to encourage entrepreneurs, and enact a comprehensive [[tax]] overhaul. Reforms in the more politically sensitive areas of structural reform and land privatisation are still lagging. Outside institutions - particularly the [[IMF]] - have encouraged Ukraine to quicken the pace and scope of reforms and have threatened to withdraw financial support. The [[Gross Domestic Product|GDP]] in [[2000]] showed strong export-based growth of 6% - the first growth since independence - and industrial production grew 12.9%. The economy continued to expand in [[2001]] as real GDP rose 9% and industrial output grew by over 14%. Growth was undergirded by strong domestic demand and growing consumer and investor confidence. == Demographics == ''Main article: [[Demographics of Ukraine]]'' Ethnic [[Ukrainians]] make up about 75% of the total population, ethnic [[Russia]]ns number about 20%. The industrial regions in the east and southeast are the most heavily populated, and about 70% of the population is urban. [[Ukrainian language|Ukrainian]] (the official state language) and [[Russian language|Russian]] are the principal languages and although Russian is very widely spoken most of the population identifies Ukrainian as their native language. Other minorities include small groups of [[Belarussian]]s, [[Moldovan]]s, [[Crimea]]n [[Tatars]], [[Bulgarians]], [[Magyars|Hungarians]], [[Romanians]], [[Poles]] and [[Jews]]. The dominant religions are the [[Ukrainian Orthodox Church]], an [[Eastern Orthodox]] church, and the [[Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church]], which practices eastern Christian rites but recognises the Roman [[Pope]] as head of the church. The largest part of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church belongs to the [[Moscow Patriarchy]]; however, following Ukrainian independence a separate [[Kiev Patriarchy]] also was established, which declared independence from Moscow. In addition to these, there also is a [[Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church]], as well as smaller [[Roman Catholic]], [[Protestant]], [[Judaism|Jewish]] and [[Moslem]] communities. == Culture == ''Main article: [[Culture of Ukraine]]'' * [http://pages.prodigy.net/l.hodges/ukraine.htm Ukrainian Language, Culture and Travel Page] * [[List of Ukrainians|List of famous Ukrainians]] * [[Music of Ukraine]] == Miscellaneous topics == * [[Communications in Ukraine]] * [[Transportation in Ukraine]] * [[Military of Ukraine]] * [[Foreign relations of Ukraine]] * [[Tourism in Ukraine]] * [[List of cities in Ukraine]] * [[Chernobyl accident]] * [[Marchlewszczyzna|Polish Autonomous Region]] == External links == * [http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en Government Portal] - Official governmental portal * [http://www.rada.gov.ua Verkhovna Rada] - Official parliamentary site (in Ukrainian) * [http://www.ukrtelecom.ua/en/offers/web_cam/ Web cam shots for selected cities across Ukraine] {{msg:Europe}} {{msg:Commonwealth_of_Independent_States}} [[da:Ukraine]] [[de:Ukraine]] [[eo:Ukrainio]] [[fi:Ukraina]] [[fr:Ukraine]] [[ja:ウクライナ]] [[ko:우크라이나]] [[nds:Ukraine]] [[nl:Oekraine]] [[pl:Ukraina]] [[uk:Україна]] [[ru:Украина]] [[sl:Ukrajina]] [[sv:Ukraina]] [[zh:乌克兰]]

[[August 24]], [[1991]] |- | [[Currency]] || [[Hryvnia]] |- | [[Time zone]] || [[Coordinated Universal Time|UTC]] +2 |- | [[National anthem]] || [[Shche ne vmerla Ukrainy ni slava ni volia]] |- | [[Top-level domain|Internet TLD]] || .UA |- | [[List_of_country_calling_codes|Calling Code]] || 380 |} == History == ''Main article: [[History of Ukraine]]'' The current territory of what is today the nation of Ukraine was a southern part of the first [[Slavic]] state, [[Kievan Rus']]. Its capital was [[Kyiv]], the capital of the modern Ukraine. Kievan Rus' was founded by [[Vikings]], or so-called [[Varangian]]s, from the area that later became Sweden. Varangians later became assimilated to the local population of Rus' ([[Ruthenian]]/Ukrainian) and gave Ukraine its first powerful dynasty, the [[Rurik]] dynasty. During the [[10th century|10th]] and [[11th century|11th centuries]] the territory of Ukraine became the center of the most important state in [[Europe]]--laying the foundation for Ukrainian national identity through subsequent centuries. Eventually, Kievan Rus' became weakened by internal quarrels and [[Mongol]] invasions. On Ukrainian territory, the state of Kievan Rus' was succeeded by the principalities of [[Halych]] and [[Volhynia|Volodymyr-Volynskii]], which were merged into the state of [[Halych-Volynia]], separated and eventually conquered by Poland and Lithuania (see the [[Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth]]). During the mid-[[17th century]] a Ukrainian state, the [[Cossack Hetmanate]], was established in central Ukraine, which remained for well over a century despite continuous [[Muscovy|Muscovite]] pressure. However, eventually Ukraine was split between the neighboring states of Poland and Russia. During the latter part of the [[18th century]], the central and eastern parts of Ukrainian ethnographic territory were annexed by the [[Russian Empire]], with the west becoming part of [[Austria-Hungary]] after the [[Partitions of Poland|partitions of Poland]]. Following the [[Russian Revolution]] in [[1917]], Ukraine was briefly independent, but not united, until [[1920]]. By [[1922]] it was split between [[Poland]] and [[Soviet Union]]. The Soviet Ukraine experienced two [[famine]]s ([[1921]]-[[1922]] and [[1932]]-[[1933]]) - the second of which was deliberate, and termed the "[[Holodomor]]" - in which many millions died (scholarly estimates range from 4 to 10 million dead). At the onset of the [[World War II]], in [[1939]], the [[Soviet Union]] invaded [[Poland]] and incorporated the Westen Ukraine into the [[Ukrainian SSR]]. In [[1941]] the German invaders and their [[Axis]] allies crushed the [[Red Army]]. In the encirclement battle of [[Kiev]], acclaimed by the Soviets as a "[[Hero City]]," more than 660,000 Soviet troops were taken captive. Initially, the Germans were warmly received as "liberators" by the Ukrainian population on the borders. Soon, however, the Germans began their bloody regime of genocide, killing and deporting Jews and Ukrainian civilians and burning down entire villages, leading many Ukrainians to conclude that Nazi rule was even worse than the Soviet regime which had killed 4 to 10 million of the Ukrainian nation, albeit over a longer period of time. Total civilian losses during the War and German occupation in Ukraine are estimated at 7 million, including over half a million Jews shot and killed by the [[Einsatzgruppen]]. Of the estimated 11 million Soviet troops who fell in battle against the Nazis, about a fourth (2.7 million) were ethnic Ukrainians. Thus, the Ukrainian nation is distinguished as the first nation to fight the Axis powers during WW II in [[Carpatho-Ukraine]] and one that saw one of the greatest bloodsheds during the War. After the WWII, the borders of then Soviet Ukraine were extended to the West, (as stipulated in the [[Hitler]]-[[Stalin]] Pact, see also [[Curzon line]]), uniting most Ukrainians under one political state. In 1954, [[Crimea]] was transferred from [[RSFSR]] to Ukraine (Crimea has no continuous land bridge to the Russian Federation.) This decision of [[Nikita Khrushchev]], intended to commemorate the 300th anniversary of the controversial [[Treaty of Pereyaslav]], seen in Soviet historiography as the 'union of two fraternal peoples', led to tensions between Russia and Ukraine after the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]]. Renewed independence was achieved in [[1991]] with the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]] and Ukraine was a founding member of the [[Commonwealth of Independent States]]. == Politics == ''Main article: [[Politics of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is a parliamentary democracy with separate [[executive]], [[judiciary|judicial]], and [[legislative]] branches. The [[president]], elected by popular vote for a five-year term, nominates the [[prime minister]] as well as the rest of the [[cabinet (government)|cabinet]], who must be confirmed by the parliament. The Ukrainian [[parliament]] is the [[unicameral]] Supreme Council or ''Verkhovna Rada'' which holds 450 seats, 225 of which are allocated on a proportional basis to those parties that gain 4% or more of the national electoral vote; the other 225 members are elected by popular vote in single-mandate constituencies. All members serve four-year terms and the parliament initiates legislation, ratifies international agreements, and approves the budget. The national flag of Ukraine represents the blue sky over the wheat fields of the [[steppe]]s. The two colors have long been used as banners for the Ukrainian people. == Regions == ''Main article: [[Regions of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is subdivided into 24 regions (''oblasti'', singular - ''oblast''), 1 autonomous republic (''avtomna respublika'') in the [[Crimea]], and 2 municipalities (''mista'', singular - ''misto'') with regional status, marked by a *:

Summers are warm across the greater part of the country, but generally hot in the south. == Economy == ''Main article: [[Economy of Ukraine]]'' Formerly an important [[agriculture|agricultural]] and industrial region of the [[Soviet Union]], Ukraine now depends on [[Russia]] for most energy supplies, especially [[natural gas]], and the lack of significant structural reform have made the Ukrainian economy vulnerable to external shocks. After [[1991]] the government liberalised most prices and erected a legal framework for [[privatisation]], but widespread resistance to reform within the government and the legislature soon stalled reform efforts and led to some backtracking. Output by [[1999]] had fallen to less than 40% the [[1991]] level. Loose monetary policies pushed [[inflation]] to [[hyperinflation]]ary levels in late [[1993]]. The current government has pledged to reduce the number of government agencies, streamline the regulatory process, create a legal environment to encourage entrepreneurs, and enact a comprehensive [[tax]] overhaul. Reforms in the more politically sensitive areas of structural reform and land privatisation are still lagging. Outside institutions - particularly the [[IMF]] - have encouraged Ukraine to quicken the pace and scope of reforms and have threatened to withdraw financial support. The [[Gross Domestic Product|GDP]] in [[2000]] showed strong export-based growth of 6% - the first growth since independence - and industrial production grew 12.9%. The economy continued to expand in [[2001]] as real GDP rose 9% and industrial output grew by over 14%. Growth was undergirded by strong domestic demand and growing consumer and investor confidence. == Demographics == ''Main article: [[Demographics of Ukraine]]'' Ethnic [[Ukrainians]] make up about 75% of the total population, ethnic [[Russia]]ns number about 20%. The industrial regions in the east and southeast are the most heavily populated, and about 70% of the population is urban. [[Ukrainian language|Ukrainian]] (the official state language) and [[Russian language|Russian]] are the principal languages and although Russian is very widely spoken most of the population identifies Ukrainian as their native language. Other minorities include small groups of [[Belarusians]], [[Moldovan]]s, [[Crimea]]n [[Tatars]], [[Bulgarians]], [[Magyars|Hungarians]], [[Romanians]], [[Poles]] and [[Jews]]. The dominant religions are the [[Ukrainian Orthodox Church]], an [[Eastern Orthodox]] church, and the [[Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church]], which practices eastern Christian rites but recognises the Roman [[Pope]] as head of the church. The largest part of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church belongs to the [[Moscow Patriarchy]]; however, following Ukrainian independence a separate [[Kiev Patriarchy]] also was established, which declared independence from Moscow. In addition to these, there also is a [[Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church]], as well as smaller [[Roman Catholic]], [[Protestant]], [[Judaism|Jewish]] and [[Moslem]] communities. == Culture == ''Main article: [[Culture of Ukraine]]'' * [http://pages.prodigy.net/l.hodges/ukraine.htm Ukrainian Language, Culture and Travel Page] * [[List of Ukrainians|List of famous Ukrainians]] * [[Music of Ukraine]] == Miscellaneous topics == * [[Communications in Ukraine]] * [[Transportation in Ukraine]] * [[Military of Ukraine]] * [[Foreign relations of Ukraine]] * [[Tourism in Ukraine]] * [[List of cities in Ukraine]] * [[Chernobyl accident]] * [[Marchlewszczyzna|Polish Autonomous Region]] == External links == * [http://www.kmu.gov.ua/control/en Government Portal] - Official governmental portal * [http://www.rada.gov.ua Verkhovna Rada] - Official parliamentary site (in Ukrainian) * [http://www.ukrtelecom.ua/en/offers/web_cam/ Web cam shots for selected cities across Ukraine] * [http://www.infoukes.com Infoukes] - General info on Ukraine's History and Politics {{msg:Commonwealth_of_Independent_States}} {{msg:Europe}} [[da:Ukraine]] [[de:Ukraine]] [[es:Ucrania]] [[eo:Ukrainio]] [[fi:Ukraina]] [[fr:Ukraine]] [[ja:ウクライナ]] [[ko:우크라이나]] [[nds:Ukraine]] [[nl:Oekraine]] [[no:Ukraina]] [[pl:Ukraina]] [[uk:Україна]] [[ru:Украина]] [[sl:Ukrajina]] [[sv:Ukraina]] [[wa:Oucrinne]] [[zh:乌克兰]]

'''Ukraine''', formerly '''The Ukraine''' (when as a part of [[USSR]]), is a country in eastern [[Europe]] which borders the [[Black Sea]] to the south, the [[Russian Federation]] to the east, [[Belarus]] to the north and [[Poland]], [[Slovakia]], [[Hungary]], [[Romania]] and [[Moldova]] to the west. {| border="1" cellpadding="2" cellspacing="0" align="right" width="280px" |+'''Україна

[[August 24]], [[1991]] |- | [[Currency]] || [[Hryvnia]] |- | [[Time zone]] || [[Coordinated Universal Time|UTC]] +2 |- | [[National anthem]] || [[Shche ne vmerla Ukraina]] |- | [[Top-level domain|Internet TLD]] || .UA |- | [[List_of_country_calling_codes|Calling Code]] || 380 |} == History == ''Main article: [[History of Ukraine]]'' The current territory of what is today the nation of Ukraine was a southern part of the first [[Slavic]] state, [[Kievan Rus']]. Its capital was [[Kyiv]], the capital of the modern Ukraine. Kievan Rus' was founded by [[Vikings]], or so-called [[Varangian]]s, from the area that later became Sweden. Varangians later became assimilated to the local population of Rus' ([[Ruthenians|Ruthenian]]/Ukrainian) and gave Ukraine its first powerful dynasty, the [[Rurik]] dynasty. During the [[10th century|10th]] and [[11th century|11th centuries]] the territory of Ukraine became the center of the most important state in [[Europe]]--laying the foundation for Ukrainian national identity through subsequent centuries. Eventually, Kievan Rus' became weakened by internal quarrels and [[Mongol]] invasions. On Ukrainian territory, the state of Kievan Rus' was succeeded by the principalities of [[Halych]] and [[Volhynia|Volodymyr-Volynskii]], which were merged into the state of [[Halych-Volynia]], separated and eventually conquered by Poland and Lithuania (see the [[Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth]]). During the mid-[[17th century]] a Ukrainian state, the [[Cossack Hetmanate]], was established in central Ukraine, which remained for well over a century despite continuous [[Muscovy|Muscovite]] pressure. However, eventually Ukraine was split between the neighboring states of Poland and Russia. Ukrainians played an important role in continuous wars between East-European monarchies and Osman Empire. Since then Ukrainian territory is widely concidered as a perrenial "bridge&buffer" between Europe and Asia (the Great Steppe). It is even fixed in the ethnic self-name ("Ukrayina" from "Okrayina, Kray" - meaning "edge"). During the latter part of the [[18th century]], the central and eastern parts of Ukrainian ethnographic territory were annexed by the [[Russian Empire]], with the west becoming part of [[Austria-Hungary]] after the [[Partitions of Poland|partitions of Poland]]. Following the [[Russian Revolution]] in [[1917]], Ukraine was briefly independent, but not united, until [[1920]]. By [[1922]] it was split between [[Poland]] and [[Soviet Union]]. The Soviet Ukraine experienced two [[famine]]s ([[1921]]-[[1922]] and [[1932]]-[[1933]]) - the second of which was deliberate, and termed the "[[Holodomor]]" - in which many millions died (scholarly estimates range from 4 to 10 million dead). At the onset of the [[World War II]], in [[1939]], the [[Soviet Union]] invaded [[Poland]] and incorporated the Westen Ukraine into the [[Ukrainian SSR]]. In [[1941]] the German invaders and their [[Axis]] allies crushed the [[Red Army]]. In the encirclement battle of [[Kiev]], acclaimed by the Soviets as a "[[Hero City]]," more than 660,000 Soviet troops were taken captive. Initially, the Germans were warmly received as "liberators" by the much of the Ukrainian population. Soon, however, the Germans began their bloody regime of genocide, killing and deporting Jews and Ukrainian civilians and burning down entire villages, leading many Ukrainians to conclude that Nazi rule was just as terrible, or even worse, than the Soviet regime which had killed 4 to 10 million of the Ukrainian nation, albeit over a longer period of time. Total civilian losses during the War and German occupation in Ukraine are estimated at 7 million, including over half a million Jews shot and killed by the [[Einsatzgruppen]]. Of the estimated 11 million Soviet troops who fell in battle against the Nazis, about a fourth (2.7 million) were ethnic Ukrainians. Thus, the Ukrainian nation is distinguished as the first nation to fight the Axis powers during WW II in [[Carpatho-Ukraine]] and one that saw one of the greatest bloodsheds during the War. After the WWII, the borders of then Soviet Ukraine were extended to the West, (as stipulated in the [[Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact]], see also [[Curzon line]]), uniting most Ukrainians under one political state. In 1954, [[Crimea]] was transferred from [[RSFSR]] to Ukraine (Crimea has no continuous land bridge to the Russian Federation.) This decision of [[Nikita Khrushchev]], intended to commemorate the 300th anniversary of the controversial [[Treaty of Pereyaslav]], seen in Soviet historiography as the 'union of two fraternal peoples', led to tensions between Russia and Ukraine after the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]]. Renewed independence was achieved in [[1991]] with the [[collapse of the Soviet Union]] and Ukraine was a founding member of the [[Commonwealth of Independent States]]. == Politics == ''Main article: [[Politics of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is a parliamentary democracy with separate [[executive]], [[judiciary|judicial]], and [[legislative]] branches. The [[president]], elected by popular vote for a five-year term, nominates the [[prime minister]] as well as the rest of the [[cabinet (government)|cabinet]], who must be confirmed by the parliament. The Ukrainian [[parliament]] is the [[unicameral]] Supreme Council or ''Verkhovna Rada'' which holds 450 seats, 225 of which are allocated on a proportional basis to those parties that gain 4% or more of the national electoral vote; the other 225 members are elected by popular vote in single-mandate constituencies. All members serve four-year terms and the parliament initiates legislation, ratifies international agreements, and approves the budget. The national flag of Ukraine represents the blue sky over the wheat fields of the [[steppe]]s. The two colors have long been used as banners for the Ukrainian people. == Regions == ''Main article: [[Regions of Ukraine]]'' Ukraine is subdivided into 24 regions (''oblasti'', singular - ''oblast''), 1 autonomous republic (''avtomna respublika'') in the [[Crimea]], and 2 municipalities (''mista'', singular - ''misto'') with regional status, marked by a *:
* [[Cherkasy region|Cherkasy]] * [[Chernihiv region|Chernihiv]] * [[Chernivtsi region|Chernivtsi]] * [[Dnipropetrovs'k region|Dnipropetrovs'k]] * [[Donets'k region|Donets'k]] * [[Ivano-Frankivs'k region|Ivano-Frankivs'k]] * [[Kharkiv region|Kharkiv]] * [[Kherson region|Kherson]] * [[Khmel'nyts'kyy region|Khmel'nyts'kyy]] * [[Kirovohrad region|Kirovohrad]] * [[Kiev]] * * [[Kiev region]] * [[Luhans'k region|Luhans'k]] * [[L'viv region|L'viv]] * [[Mykolayiv region|Mykolayiv]] * [[Odessa region|Odessa]] * [[Poltava region|Poltava]] * [[Republic of Crimea]] * [[Rivne region|Rivne]] * [[Sevastopol]] * * [[Sumy region|Sumy]] * [[Ternopil' region|Ternopil']] * [[Vinnytsya pregion|Vinnytsya]] * [[Volyn region|Volyn]] * [[Zakarpattya region|Zakarpattya]] * [[Zaporizhzhya region|Zaporizhzhya]] * [[Zhytomyr region|Zhytomyr]]

To the southwest the [[river delta|delta]] of the [[Danube]] forms the border with Romania. Mountains are found only in the western range of the [[Carpathian Mountains]], of which the highest is the [[Hora Hoverla]] at 2,061 m, and in the [[Crimea]]n peninsula in the extreme south along the coast. The [[Gross Domestic Product|GDP]] in [[2000]] showed strong export-based growth of 6% - the first growth since independence - and industrial production grew 12.9%. Varangians later became assimilated to the local population of Rus' ([[Ruthenians|Ruthenian]]/Ukrainian) and gave Ukraine its first powerful dynasty, the [[Rurik]] dynasty. During the [[10th century|10th]] and [[11th century|11th centuries]] the territory of Ukraine became the center of the most important state in [[Europe]]--laying the foundation for Ukrainian national identity through subsequent centuries. The term "Rus'" refered to all of the Slavic principalities in the region (Rus' Chervona for a example). [[Kyiv]], and [[Kievian Rus']] were the seat of the Grand prince of the Rurik Dynasty. The ruler of Kyiv was also in effect the ruler of all the Rus' principalities. Kyivian Rus' declined during the Mongol invasions, and the seat of the Grand Prince was moved first to Vladimir and then to Moscow. Eventually, [[Kyivan Rus']] became weakened by internal quarrels and [[Mongol]] invasions.
